name: cold-email
description: Write cold emails that get replies using proven frameworks (AIDA, PAS, BAB). Enforces best practices like 50-125 word limit and personalized openers.
capabilities: Generate cold emails using AIDA (Awareness-Interest-Desire-Action) framework
Enforce 50-125 word count limits for optimal response rates
Generate personalized subject lines (3-5 words, lowercase, no clickbait)
Apply seven hard rules: word count, subject format, personalized openers, no fluff, single CTA, under 30-second read time, mobile-friendly formatting
author: humanizerai
securitySummary: All static analysis findings are false positives. The skill is a pure text-generation prompt with no code execution capabilities. Lines flagged as 'external_commands' are markdown code blocks showing email output format, not shell commands. The skill provides copywriting frameworks for cold emails and enforces writing best practices (word count, personalization). No security risks detected.
valueStatement: Cold emails that fail to convert waste time and damage sender reputation. This skill uses proven copywriting frameworks (AIDA, PAS, BAB) to generate personalized, response-driven emails that respect best practices like 50-125 word limits and specific CTAs.
userTitle: Write Cold Emails That Get Replies
limitations: Cannot research prospects or gather personalization data automatically
Does not send emails or integrate with email providers
Cannot track open rates, click rates, or response metrics
Framework selection requires user judgment about target audience and context

useCases.targetUser: Sales representatives, business development representatives, account executives
useCases.title: Sales Outreach
useCases.description: Sales professionals generating personalized cold emails to prospects to request meetings, demos, or product discussions

useCases.targetUser: Job seekers, career changers, entrepreneurs, researchers
useCases.title: Networking Requests
useCases.description: Professionals reaching out to industry peers, potential mentors, or conference speakers for informational interviews or advice

useCases.targetUser: Startup founders, business owners, partnership managers, investor relations
useCases.title: Partnership Proposals
useCases.description: Business owners and founders contacting potential partners, collaborators, or investors to explore strategic relationships

promptTemplates.title: Basic Sales Email
promptTemplates.prompt: Write a cold email to the VP of Marketing at a SaaS company. Goal: Request a 15-minute demo of our analytics platform. Context: They recently published a blog post about data-driven marketing challenges.
promptTemplates.scenario: Sales rep targeting marketing leadership with relevant content context

promptTemplates.title: Networking Request
promptTemplates.prompt: Draft a cold email to a senior engineer at Google I admire. Goal: Request a 20-minute informational interview about their career path. Context: I read their recent article on distributed systems and it helped me understand consensus algorithms.
promptTemplates.scenario: Junior professional seeking mentorship from industry expert

promptTemplates.title: Partnership Inquiry
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a cold email to the CEO of a complementary fintech startup. Goal: Explore a strategic partnership opportunity. Context: Our payment processing product could integrate with their expense tracking software to create a unified financial platform for small businesses.
promptTemplates.scenario: Founder proposing strategic partnership to create integrated solution

promptTemplates.title: Guest Blog Pitch
promptTemplates.prompt: Write a cold email to the editor of a major tech publication. Goal: Pitch a guest article about AI ethics in enterprise software. Context: I noticed they don't have coverage on this specific angle and I have 10 years of enterprise AI implementation experience.
promptTemplates.scenario: Subject matter expert pitching thought leadership content

bestPractices: Always provide specific context or personalization hooks - generic templates perform 40% worse than personalized messages
Match the framework to your goal: AIDA for product sales, PAS for pain-point solutions, BAB for transformational offers
Test different subject line approaches - questions, statistics, and specific references each work for different audiences
antiPatterns: Don't bypass the word count limits - emails over 125 words see response rates drop below 5%
Avoid vague CTAs like 'let me know if you're interested' - specific requests (15-minute call, feedback on X) perform 3x better
Never use template-sounding phrases like 'I hope this email finds you well' or 'picking this up from my previous email' - these trigger spam filters and reduce credibility

faq.question: Which framework works best for B2B sales?
faq.answer: AIDA typically performs best for B2B sales because it builds logical progression from awareness to action. However, use PAS when you know a specific pain point the prospect is experiencing, or BAB when you can clearly contrast their current problems with your solution's outcome.

faq.question: Can I use this for follow-up emails?
faq.answer: This skill is designed for initial cold emails. For follow-ups, use the /follow-up skill which handles multi-message sequences, timing strategies, and different approaches for non-responders. Cold emails and follow-ups require different psychological approaches.

faq.question: Why the 50-125 word limit?
faq.answer: Data from 100,000+ cold emails shows peak response rates in this range. Under 50 words feels like spam or lacks credibility. Over 125 words requires too much cognitive load and decision fatigue sets in. The sweet spot is 75-100 words for most B2B contexts.

faq.question: How do I get personalization data?
faq.answer: This skill uses the context you provide. Research the prospect on LinkedIn, company blogs, recent news, Twitter, or mutual connections. Even one specific detail (their recent post, company announcement, shared connection) doubles response rates compared to generic outreach.

faq.question: Does this work for consumer emails or just B2B?
faq.answer: The frameworks work for both contexts, but the tone and approach differ. B2B requires more formality and logical proof. Consumer emails can be more casual and emotion-driven. Adjust the examples and language based on whether you're selling to businesses or individual consumers.

faq.question: Can I request meetings longer than 15 minutes?
faq.answer: You can, but 15-minute requests have 3x higher acceptance rates than 30-minute requests. Once you're in the conversation, you can extend. The goal is to start the relationship, not close the entire deal in one meeting. Many successful cold emails use 'coffee chat' framing to reduce commitment pressure.
